title: Docker Compose Basics and Multi-Container Setups
text:
Docker Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications. Using a docker-compose.yml file, you declare all the services your application needs, including their images, environment variables, port mappings, and volume mounts. Running `docker compose up` brings the entire stack to life in the correct order, respecting dependency chains defined with `depends_on`.

Networking in Docker Compose is handled automatically. By default, Compose creates a single network for your application and each service container joins that network. Containers can reach each other using the service name as a hostname. For example, if you have a `web` service and a `db` service, your web container can connect to the database at `db:5432`. You can also define custom networks to isolate groups of services or connect to external networks.

A typical multi-container setup might include an ASP.NET Core web application, a PostgreSQL database, a Redis cache, and an Nginx reverse proxy. Each service is defined with its own build context or image reference, resource limits, restart policies, and health checks. Environment-specific overrides can be layered using `docker-compose.override.yml` files, making it straightforward to maintain different configurations for development, staging, and production.

Compose also supports scaling services with the `--scale` flag, though for production orchestration most teams graduate to Kubernetes. The `docker compose watch` command introduced in recent versions enables live file synchronization during development, automatically rebuilding and restarting containers when source files change.

title: Kubernetes Pod Management, Deployments, and Scaling
text:
Kubernetes pods are the smallest deployable units in a cluster. A pod encapsulates one or more containers that share the same network namespace, IP address, and storage volumes. While you can run individual pods directly, production workloads are almost always managed through higher-level controllers like Deployments, StatefulSets, or DaemonSets that handle pod lifecycle, scaling, and self-healing.

Deployments are the most common controller for stateless applications. A Deployment spec declares the desired number of replicas, the pod template (including container images, resource requests, and limits), and the update strategy. When you update the container image tag in a Deployment, Kubernetes performs a rolling update by default, gradually replacing old pods with new ones while maintaining availability. You can configure `maxSurge` and `maxUnavailable` to control the rollout pace.

Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) adjusts the number of pod replicas based on observed CPU utilization, memory usage, or custom metrics. For example, you might configure an HPA to scale your web frontend between 3 and 20 replicas, targeting 70% average CPU utilization. The Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) complements this by recommending or automatically adjusting resource requests and limits for individual containers.

Pod disruption budgets (PDBs) ensure that voluntary disruptions like node drains or cluster upgrades do not take down too many pods simultaneously. By setting `minAvailable` or `maxUnavailable` in a PDB, you guarantee that a minimum number of pods remain running during maintenance operations. Combined with pod anti-affinity rules, these mechanisms provide robust high-availability configurations.

title: Container Networking: Bridge, Overlay, and Host Networks
text:
Docker provides several network drivers that control how containers communicate with each other and the outside world. The default bridge network creates an isolated network namespace with its own subnet. Containers attached to the same bridge can communicate via IP addresses, and user-defined bridge networks additionally enable DNS-based service discovery using container names. Port mapping with `-p` exposes container ports on the host.

Overlay networks span multiple Docker hosts and are essential for Docker Swarm and multi-host deployments. They use VXLAN encapsulation to tunnel container traffic across the underlying physical network. When you create an overlay network with `docker network create --driver overlay my-overlay`, any service attached to it can communicate with other services on the same overlay, regardless of which physical host the containers run on. Overlay networks support encryption of data in transit using the `--opt encrypted` flag.

The host network driver removes network isolation between the container and the Docker host. Containers using `--network host` share the host's network namespace directly, which eliminates NAT overhead and can improve performance for latency-sensitive applications. However, this means port conflicts must be managed manually, and you lose the security benefits of network isolation.

Macvlan networks assign a MAC address to each container, making them appear as physical devices on the network. This is useful when integrating with legacy systems or network monitoring tools that expect real MAC addresses. For Kubernetes, the Container Network Interface (CNI) plugin model provides extensible networking through implementations like Calico, Flannel, Cilium, and Weave Net, each offering different tradeoffs between performance, policy enforcement, and encryption capabilities.

title: Private Docker Registry Setup, Authentication, and Caching
text:
Running a private Docker registry gives your organization full control over image storage, access policies, and distribution. The official Docker Registry image (registry:2) can be deployed as a single container for small teams or behind a load balancer for high availability. Basic setup involves running `docker run -d -p 5000:5000 --name registry registry:2`, but production deployments require TLS certificates, persistent storage, and authentication.

Authentication for private registries is typically handled through HTTP basic auth with an htpasswd file, token-based authentication using an external authorization server, or integration with an identity provider via OAuth2. The registry configuration file (config.yml) controls storage backends, access logging, and notification webhooks. For storage, you can use the local filesystem, Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, or Google Cloud Storage as the backing store for image layers.

Image caching and pull-through proxies significantly reduce bandwidth usage and improve pull times. Configuring your private registry as a pull-through cache for Docker Hub means that the first pull of a public image is fetched from Docker Hub and cached locally. Subsequent pulls from any machine in your network are served from the local registry. This is especially valuable in CI/CD environments where the same base images are pulled repeatedly across many build agents.

Harbor and GitLab Container Registry are popular open-source alternatives that add vulnerability scanning, image signing with Notary, role-based access control, and replication between registries in different data centers. For garbage collection of unused image layers, run `docker exec registry bin/registry garbage-collect /etc/docker/registry/config.yml` periodically to reclaim storage space.

title: Docker Volumes, Persistent Storage, and Bind Mounts
text:
Docker volumes are the preferred mechanism for persisting data generated by and used by Docker containers. Unlike bind mounts, volumes are fully managed by Docker and stored in a dedicated area on the host filesystem (typically /var/lib/docker/volumes/). They can be created explicitly with `docker volume create my-volume` or implicitly when a container references a named volume. Volumes survive container removal, making them essential for databases, file uploads, and any stateful workload.

Bind mounts map a specific host directory or file into the container. They are useful during development when you want source code changes on the host to be immediately reflected inside the container. For example, `docker run -v $(pwd)/src:/app/src myimage` maps the local src directory into the container. However, bind mounts have tighter coupling to the host filesystem structure and may cause permission issues when the container process runs as a different UID than the host user.

For production database containers, named volumes with explicit backup strategies are recommended. A common pattern is to run PostgreSQL with a named volume for the data directory: `docker run -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres:16`. Backup can be performed by running pg_dump inside the container or by using volume snapshot capabilities provided by cloud storage drivers. Docker volume plugins extend storage options to include NFS, Ceph, GlusterFS, and cloud provider block storage.

tmpfs mounts are an additional option that stores data in memory only. They are suitable for sensitive information that should not persist to disk, such as temporary credentials or secrets. Unlike volumes and bind mounts, tmpfs mounts are cleared when the container stops. In Docker Compose, all these mount types can be declared in the `volumes` section of a service definition using either the short syntax (`volume_name:/container/path`) or the long syntax with explicit type, source, and target fields.

title: PostgreSQL Backup Strategies: pg_dump, WAL Archiving, and PITR
text:
PostgreSQL offers several backup approaches, each suited to different recovery requirements. Logical backups with pg_dump produce SQL scripts or custom-format archives that can restore individual databases or tables. Running `pg_dump -Fc -f backup.dump mydb` creates a compressed custom-format backup that supports parallel restore with `pg_restore -j 4 -d mydb backup.dump`. For backing up an entire cluster including roles and tablespaces, pg_dumpall is the appropriate tool.

WAL (Write-Ahead Log) archiving enables continuous backup and point-in-time recovery (PITR). By setting `archive_mode = on` and configuring `archive_command` in postgresql.conf, PostgreSQL copies completed WAL segments to an archive location. Combined with a base backup taken using `pg_basebackup`, you can restore the database to any point in time by replaying WAL files up to the desired recovery target. This is configured in the recovery.conf (or postgresql.auto.conf in newer versions) with `recovery_target_time` or `recovery_target_lsn`.

Tools like pgBackRest and Barman build on these primitives to provide enterprise-grade backup management. pgBackRest supports incremental and differential backups, parallel backup and restore, backup encryption, and S3-compatible object storage as a repository. It manages WAL archiving automatically and can verify backup integrity without performing a full restore. Barman offers similar capabilities with a focus on remote backup management across multiple PostgreSQL servers.

A robust backup strategy typically combines daily base backups with continuous WAL archiving, retaining enough history to recover from data corruption that might not be noticed immediately. Testing restores regularly is critical. Backup retention policies, monitoring for archive lag, and encryption of backup files at rest and in transit are all essential components of a production backup plan.

title: MySQL to PostgreSQL Migration Steps and Data Type Mapping
text:
Migrating from MySQL to PostgreSQL requires careful planning around schema conversion, data type mapping, and application query compatibility. The first step is auditing the MySQL schema to identify data types, auto-increment columns, stored procedures, triggers, and MySQL-specific syntax that needs conversion. Tools like pgloader can automate much of this process, reading directly from a MySQL source and writing to PostgreSQL while handling type conversions.

Data type mapping between the two systems requires attention to several differences. MySQL's TINYINT(1) used for booleans maps to PostgreSQL's native BOOLEAN type. AUTO_INCREMENT becomes SERIAL or the modern GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY. MySQL's ENUM type can be converted to PostgreSQL's CREATE TYPE ... AS ENUM or replaced with a CHECK constraint. DATETIME maps to TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE, while MySQL's DOUBLE maps to DOUBLE PRECISION. TEXT and BLOB types have direct equivalents, though PostgreSQL's BYTEA replaces BLOB for binary data.

Query syntax differences are a significant source of migration issues. MySQL uses backticks for identifier quoting while PostgreSQL uses double quotes. String concatenation uses CONCAT() in MySQL but the || operator in PostgreSQL. MySQL's LIMIT with OFFSET syntax is compatible, but INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE must be rewritten to use PostgreSQL's INSERT ... ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE. The GROUP BY behavior also differs: PostgreSQL strictly requires that all selected columns either appear in GROUP BY or are used with aggregate functions.

A phased migration approach reduces risk. Start by running both databases in parallel with writes going to MySQL and being replicated to PostgreSQL. Validate data consistency using row counts and checksums. Gradually shift read traffic to PostgreSQL while monitoring query performance. Tools like AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) or custom CDC pipelines based on Debezium can facilitate this dual-write phase. Only cut over writes to PostgreSQL after thorough validation.

title: Database Connection Pooling with PgBouncer and Npgsql
text:
Connection pooling is essential for applications that handle many concurrent database requests. Opening a new PostgreSQL connection involves TCP handshake, TLS negotiation, and authentication, which can take 50-100ms. A connection pool maintains a set of open connections that are reused across requests, dramatically reducing latency and preventing the database from being overwhelmed by connection storms.

PgBouncer is a lightweight connection pooler that sits between your application and PostgreSQL. It operates in three modes: session pooling (one server connection per client session), transaction pooling (connections returned after each transaction), and statement pooling (connections returned after each statement). Transaction pooling is the most common choice for web applications. Configuration involves setting `pool_size`, `min_pool_size`, `reserve_pool_size`, and `server_lifetime` in pgbouncer.ini. A typical setup might maintain 20-50 server connections while accepting hundreds of client connections.

Npgsql, the .NET PostgreSQL driver, includes its own built-in connection pooling. Connection string parameters like `Minimum Pool Size`, `Maximum Pool Size`, `Connection Idle Lifetime`, and `Connection Pruning Interval` control pool behavior. The default maximum pool size is 100 connections per unique connection string. When all pooled connections are in use, new requests wait up to the `Timeout` period (default 15 seconds) before throwing an NpgsqlException. Monitoring pool statistics is possible through Npgsql's NpgsqlEventSource or OpenTelemetry instrumentation.

When using both PgBouncer and Npgsql pooling, be careful to configure them correctly together. In many setups, Npgsql pooling is disabled (Maximum Pool Size=1 or Pooling=false per PgBouncer target) and PgBouncer handles all pooling. Alternatively, Npgsql pools connections to PgBouncer, and PgBouncer pools connections to PostgreSQL, creating a two-tier pooling arrangement. The key metric to watch is the total number of actual PostgreSQL connections, which should stay well below `max_connections` (default 100).

title: B-tree, GIN, and GiST Indexes: Query Plans and EXPLAIN
text:
PostgreSQL supports multiple index types, each optimized for different query patterns. B-tree indexes are the default and most versatile, supporting equality and range queries on sortable data types. They work well for columns used in WHERE clauses with operators like =, <, >, BETWEEN, and IN, as well as ORDER BY clauses. A B-tree index on a high-cardinality column like a user ID or timestamp provides excellent selectivity.

GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) indexes are designed for composite values where each indexed item may contain multiple elements. They excel at full-text search (tsvector columns), JSONB containment queries (@>, ?, ?|, ?& operators), and array overlap/containment operations. Creating a GIN index with `CREATE INDEX idx_data ON docs USING GIN (data jsonb_path_ops)` dramatically speeds up queries like `SELECT * FROM docs WHERE data @> '{"status": "active"}'`. The `jsonb_path_ops` operator class is more compact than the default but only supports the @> operator.

GiST (Generalized Search Tree) indexes support geometric data types, range types, and full-text search. They are the index of choice for PostGIS spatial queries, enabling efficient bounding box searches, nearest-neighbor lookups, and intersection tests. GiST indexes also work with exclusion constraints using the EXCLUDE clause to enforce non-overlapping ranges, which is invaluable for scheduling applications.

Using EXPLAIN ANALYZE is essential for understanding query performance and verifying that indexes are being used effectively. The output shows the query planner's chosen execution strategy, including sequential scans, index scans, bitmap heap scans, nested loop joins, and hash joins. Key metrics include actual time (in milliseconds), rows processed at each step, and the number of loops. A common pattern is running `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)` to see both timing and buffer usage, helping identify whether poor performance stems from missing indexes, outdated statistics, or insufficient shared_buffers.

title: PostgreSQL Streaming Replication and Primary-Replica Setup
text:
PostgreSQL streaming replication provides high availability and read scaling by continuously shipping WAL records from a primary server to one or more replica servers. The replicas apply these records in real time, maintaining a near-identical copy of the primary's data. Setting up streaming replication involves configuring the primary to accept replication connections and configuring replicas to connect and stream WAL data.

On the primary server, modify postgresql.conf to set `wal_level = replica`, `max_wal_senders = 10` (or appropriate number), and `wal_keep_size = 1GB` to retain enough WAL segments for replicas to catch up after brief disconnections. Create a dedicated replication role with `CREATE ROLE replicator WITH REPLICATION LOGIN PASSWORD 'secret'` and add an entry to pg_hba.conf allowing the replica's IP to connect using this role with the replication database.

On the replica, use `pg_basebackup -h primary-host -D /var/lib/postgresql/16/main -U replicator -P -Xs -R` to take a base backup and automatically create the standby.signal file and configure primary_conninfo in postgresql.auto.conf. The `-R` flag is particularly important as it sets up the replica configuration automatically. After starting PostgreSQL on the replica, it connects to the primary and begins streaming WAL records.

Monitoring replication lag is critical for ensuring replicas are suitable for read queries. Query `pg_stat_replication` on the primary to see `sent_lsn`, `write_lsn`, `flush_lsn`, and `replay_lsn` for each replica. The difference between `sent_lsn` and `replay_lsn` indicates the replication lag. For synchronous replication, set `synchronous_commit = on` and `synchronous_standby_names` on the primary. This guarantees that transactions are not considered committed until at least one replica has confirmed receipt, providing zero data loss at the cost of increased write latency.

title: OAuth2 Authorization Code Flow, Token Refresh, and Scopes
text:
The OAuth2 authorization code flow is the recommended grant type for server-side web applications. The flow begins when the application redirects the user's browser to the authorization server's /authorize endpoint with parameters including client_id, redirect_uri, response_type=code, scope, and a randomly generated state parameter for CSRF protection. After the user authenticates and consents, the authorization server redirects back to the application's redirect_uri with an authorization code.

The application's backend exchanges this authorization code for tokens by making a POST request to the token endpoint. This request includes the authorization code, client_id, client_secret, grant_type=authorization_code, and the same redirect_uri. The response contains an access_token (typically short-lived, 15-60 minutes), a refresh_token (long-lived, days to months), the token_type (usually Bearer), and the expires_in value. The access token is used in API requests via the Authorization header.

Token refresh is handled transparently when the access token expires. The application sends a POST to the token endpoint with grant_type=refresh_token and the refresh token. The server responds with a new access token and optionally a new refresh token (refresh token rotation). Implementing automatic token refresh in middleware prevents disruption to the user experience. If the refresh token is expired or revoked, the user must re-authenticate through the full authorization flow.

Scopes define the specific permissions an application requests. For example, `openid profile email` requests basic identity information, while `api.read api.write` might grant API access. Scopes should follow the principle of least privilege. In ASP.NET Core, the Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.OpenIdConnect package handles the entire flow, including token refresh, token validation, and claims mapping. Configure it in Program.cs with `AddOpenIdConnect` specifying Authority, ClientId, ClientSecret, ResponseType, and the required scopes.

title: JWT Creation, Validation, Claims, and Key Rotation
text:
JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) are compact, URL-safe tokens used extensively for API authentication and authorization. A JWT consists of three Base64URL-encoded parts separated by dots: the header (algorithm and token type), the payload (claims), and the signature. Common signing algorithms include HS256 (symmetric HMAC) for simple setups and RS256 or ES256 (asymmetric RSA or ECDSA) for distributed systems where token issuers and validators are separate services.

Creating JWTs in .NET uses the System.IdentityModel.Tokens.Jwt package. A SecurityTokenDescriptor specifies the subject (ClaimsIdentity with user claims), issuer, audience, expiration time, and signing credentials. Standard claims include `sub` (subject/user ID), `iss` (issuer), `aud` (audience), `exp` (expiration), `iat` (issued at), and `jti` (unique token ID). Custom claims like roles, permissions, or tenant IDs are added as needed. Keep the payload small since JWTs are sent with every request.

Token validation in ASP.NET Core is configured through `AddAuthentication().AddJwtBearer()` in the service configuration. The TokenValidationParameters specify the valid issuer, valid audience, issuer signing key, clock skew tolerance, and whether to validate each of these. Setting `ValidateLifetime = true` ensures expired tokens are rejected. For asymmetric algorithms, the validator only needs the public key, not the private signing key.

Key rotation is essential for security hygiene. The signing key should be rotated periodically (e.g., every 90 days) without invalidating existing tokens. This is achieved by publishing the current and previous public keys at a JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) endpoint. The token header's `kid` (key ID) claim tells validators which key to use for verification. During rotation, new tokens are signed with the new key while the old key remains in the JWKS endpoint until all tokens signed with it have expired. Azure Key Vault, AWS KMS, or HashiCorp Vault can manage the key lifecycle.

title: API Key Authentication Patterns, Headers, and Rate Limiting
text:
API key authentication is a straightforward approach for server-to-server communication and third-party API access. Unlike OAuth2 tokens, API keys are long-lived credentials that identify the calling application rather than a specific user. They are typically sent in a custom HTTP header (e.g., `X-API-Key: abc123`) or as a query parameter, though headers are preferred because query strings may be logged in server access logs and browser history.

Implementing API key authentication in ASP.NET Core involves creating a custom authentication handler that extends AuthenticationHandler<AuthenticationSchemeOptions>. The handler extracts the API key from the request header, looks it up in a database or cache, and returns an AuthenticateResult with the associated claims (client name, allowed scopes, rate limit tier). A simpler approach uses middleware or an action filter that checks the key before the request reaches the controller. Store API keys as salted hashes in the database, not in plaintext.

Rate limiting is essential for API key-based access to prevent abuse and ensure fair usage. ASP.NET Core 7+ includes built-in rate limiting middleware with fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, and concurrency limiter algorithms. Each API key can be assigned a rate limit tier that determines the allowed requests per minute or hour. The rate limiter returns a 429 Too Many Requests response with Retry-After and X-RateLimit-Remaining headers when the limit is exceeded.

API key management includes generation (using cryptographically secure random bytes, typically 32-64 bytes encoded as Base64 or hex), rotation (allowing multiple active keys per client for zero-downtime rotation), revocation (immediately disabling a compromised key), and expiration (optional automatic expiry after a set period). A self-service portal where API consumers can view usage statistics, rotate keys, and manage permissions reduces operational burden on the API team.

title: CORS Configuration for ASP.NET Core, Preflight Requests, and Allowed Origins
text:
Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is a browser security mechanism that controls which domains can make requests to your API. When a web application hosted on https://app.example.com makes a fetch request to https://api.example.com, the browser enforces CORS policies by checking the response headers from the API server. Without proper CORS configuration, the browser blocks the response, even though the server processed the request successfully.

In ASP.NET Core, CORS is configured in Program.cs using `builder.Services.AddCors()` to define policies and `app.UseCors()` to apply them. A named policy specifies allowed origins, methods, and headers: `options.AddPolicy("MyPolicy", policy => policy.WithOrigins("https://app.example.com").WithMethods("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE").WithHeaders("Content-Type", "Authorization").AllowCredentials())`. The `AllowCredentials` method is needed when the frontend sends cookies or authorization headers, but it cannot be combined with `AllowAnyOrigin`.

Preflight requests are OPTIONS requests that the browser sends automatically before certain cross-origin requests. These occur when the request uses methods other than GET/HEAD/POST, includes custom headers, or uses a Content-Type other than application/x-www-form-urlencoded, multipart/form-data, or text/plain. The preflight response must include Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, and Access-Control-Allow-Headers. Setting `SetPreflightMaxAge(TimeSpan.FromMinutes(10))` tells the browser to cache preflight results, reducing the number of OPTIONS requests.

Common CORS pitfalls include using `AllowAnyOrigin` in production (a security risk), forgetting to list required custom headers in the allowed headers list, not handling the OPTIONS method in middleware order (CORS middleware must come before authorization and endpoint routing), and misconfiguring allowed origins when using subdomains or varying ports during development. For dynamic origin validation, use `SetIsOriginAllowed` with a predicate function that checks the origin against a database or configuration.

title: GitHub Actions for .NET Projects: Build, Test, and Publish
text:
GitHub Actions provides CI/CD automation directly within GitHub repositories. For .NET projects, a typical workflow file (.github/workflows/ci.yml) triggers on push and pull_request events and runs on ubuntu-latest. The workflow sets up the .NET SDK using `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` with the desired SDK version, restores NuGet packages with `dotnet restore`, builds the solution with `dotnet build --no-restore --configuration Release`, and runs tests with `dotnet test --no-build --configuration Release --logger trx --results-directory TestResults`.

Test result publishing and code coverage enhance the CI experience. After running tests, use an action like `dorny/test-reporter@v1` to parse TRX files and display test results directly in the pull request. For code coverage, add `--collect:"XPlat Code Coverage"` to the dotnet test command and use `codecov/codecov-action@v3` or `danielpalme/ReportGenerator` to generate and publish HTML coverage reports. Setting minimum coverage thresholds in the workflow ensures code quality does not regress.

NuGet package publishing is handled in a separate job that runs only on tagged commits or manual triggers. The job runs `dotnet pack --configuration Release --no-build -o ./artifacts` to create .nupkg files, then `dotnet nuget push ./artifacts/*.nupkg --api-key ${{ secrets.NUGET_API_KEY }} --source https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json` to publish. Caching NuGet packages between runs using `actions/cache@v3` with the path `~/.nuget/packages` and a hash of the lock file significantly speeds up restore times.

Matrix builds allow testing across multiple .NET versions and operating systems simultaneously. Define a strategy matrix with `dotnet-version: ['6.0.x', '8.0.x', '9.0.x']` and `os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest]` to ensure compatibility. Conditional steps using `if: matrix.os == 'ubuntu-latest'` can restrict certain operations like Docker builds to specific runners. Branch protection rules requiring these checks to pass before merging enforce quality gates.

title: Azure DevOps Pipeline YAML, Stages, and Artifacts
text:
Azure DevOps Pipelines use YAML definitions (azure-pipelines.yml) to describe build and release workflows. A pipeline consists of stages, each containing one or more jobs, which in turn contain steps. This hierarchical structure enables complex workflows like build-test-deploy with approval gates between stages. Triggers define when the pipeline runs, supporting branch filters, path filters, and scheduled cron expressions.

A typical .NET pipeline starts with a build stage that uses the `DotNetCoreCLI@2` task for restore, build, test, and publish operations. Variables and variable groups centralize configuration like solution paths, build configurations, and connection strings. Variable groups can link to Azure Key Vault secrets, keeping sensitive values out of the YAML file. Template references (`template: steps/build.yml`) enable reuse across multiple pipelines.

Artifact management in Azure DevOps uses the `PublishBuildArtifacts@1` or newer `PublishPipelineArtifact@1` task to store build outputs. Published artifacts are available for download, inspection, and consumption by subsequent stages or pipelines. For .NET applications, the build stage typically publishes the output of `dotnet publish` as an artifact, and the deployment stage downloads and deploys it. Azure Artifacts feeds provide NuGet package hosting integrated with the pipeline, supporting upstream sources from nuget.org.

Deployment stages use environment resources with approval gates and deployment strategies. Defining an environment in Azure DevOps allows you to track deployment history, configure manual approvals, and set up exclusive locks to prevent concurrent deployments. The `AzureWebApp@1` task deploys to Azure App Service, while `KubernetesManifest@1` handles Kubernetes deployments. Service connections securely store credentials for external services like Azure subscriptions, Docker registries, and Kubernetes clusters.

title: Blue-Green, Canary, and Rolling Deployment Strategies
text:
Deployment strategies determine how new application versions are released to production while minimizing downtime and risk. The simplest approach is a rolling update, where instances are gradually replaced with the new version. Kubernetes performs rolling deployments by default, replacing pods one at a time (or in configurable batches) while maintaining the desired number of running instances. Health checks ensure new pods are ready before old ones are terminated.

Blue-green deployment maintains two identical production environments: blue (current) and green (new). The new version is deployed to the green environment and thoroughly tested. Once validated, traffic is switched from blue to green by updating the load balancer, DNS record, or Kubernetes service selector. If issues are discovered, rollback is instant by switching traffic back to blue. The downside is the cost of maintaining two full environments, though cloud infrastructure makes this more feasible. Azure App Service deployment slots implement this pattern natively with the swap operation.

Canary deployments route a small percentage of traffic (e.g., 5-10%) to the new version while the majority continues using the current version. Metrics like error rates, latency percentiles, and business KPIs are monitored during the canary phase. If the new version performs well, traffic is gradually shifted (10%, 25%, 50%, 100%). If problems appear, the canary is rolled back with minimal user impact. Service mesh tools like Istio and Linkerd provide fine-grained traffic splitting based on weighted routing rules.

Feature flags complement these strategies by decoupling deployment from release. Code for new features is deployed to production but hidden behind flags that can be enabled for specific users, percentage-based rollouts, or geographic regions. This allows trunk-based development where all code flows to main and features are activated independently. Tools like LaunchDarkly, Azure App Configuration, and open-source Flagsmith provide feature flag management with audit trails and instant kill switches.

title: NuGet Package Publishing, Versioning, and Artifact Retention
text:
NuGet package management is central to .NET development, and publishing your own packages requires careful attention to versioning, metadata, and repository configuration. Packages are created with `dotnet pack`, which reads metadata from the .csproj file including PackageId, Version, Authors, Description, and PackageLicenseExpression. Central Package Management (CPM) using Directory.Packages.props ensures consistent dependency versions across a solution, with all PackageVersion entries defined in a single location.

Semantic Versioning (SemVer) guides version number decisions: increment the major version for breaking changes, minor for new backward-compatible features, and patch for bug fixes. Pre-release versions like 2.1.0-beta.1 are sorted correctly by NuGet clients and are excluded from stable version resolution by default. In CI/CD pipelines, version numbers are typically derived from Git tags or calculated by tools like Nerdbank.GitVersioning or GitVersion, which analyze commit history and branch names to determine the appropriate version.

Publishing to nuget.org uses `dotnet nuget push` with an API key stored as a CI/CD secret. For private packages, Azure Artifacts, GitHub Packages, or a self-hosted BaGet server provide authenticated NuGet feeds. Source Link integration embeds repository URLs and commit hashes in the package, enabling debuggers to automatically fetch source code. Symbol packages (.snupkg) published alongside the main package provide PDB files for step-through debugging.

Artifact retention policies prevent unbounded growth of build outputs. CI/CD systems typically retain artifacts from the last N builds or for a configurable number of days. NuGet feeds may have storage limits, and unlisting old pre-release versions keeps the feed clean. For critical packages, consider maintaining a separate stable feed with only release versions. Signed packages using `dotnet nuget sign` with a code-signing certificate verify the publisher's identity and detect tampering.

title: ASP.NET Core Configuration: appsettings, Environment Variables, and IOptions
text:
ASP.NET Core's configuration system provides a layered approach where values from multiple sources are merged, with later sources overriding earlier ones. The default ordering is appsettings.json, appsettings.{Environment}.json, environment variables, command-line arguments, and user secrets (in Development). This allows developers to define defaults in appsettings.json, override per environment, and inject secrets without committing them to source control.

The IOptions pattern provides strongly-typed access to configuration sections. Define a POCO class matching the JSON structure, then register it with `builder.Services.Configure<MyOptions>(builder.Configuration.GetSection("MySection"))`. Inject `IOptions<MyOptions>` for singleton-lifetime settings, `IOptionsSnapshot<MyOptions>` for scoped settings that refresh per request, or `IOptionsMonitor<MyOptions>` for real-time change detection. Adding `.ValidateDataAnnotations().ValidateOnStart()` ensures configuration values are valid at application startup rather than failing at runtime.

Environment variables are the preferred configuration source for containerized deployments. ASP.NET Core maps environment variables to configuration keys using a double underscore as the section separator (e.g., `ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection` maps to `ConnectionStrings:DefaultConnection`). In Docker and Kubernetes, these are set through docker-compose.yml environment sections, Kubernetes ConfigMaps, or Kubernetes Secrets mounted as environment variables.

For sensitive values, the Secret Manager tool (`dotnet user-secrets`) stores secrets in a per-project JSON file outside the source tree during development. In production, Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or HashiCorp Vault provide centralized secret management with access policies, rotation, and audit logging. The Azure Key Vault configuration provider integrates directly into the configuration pipeline, loading secrets as if they were appsettings values. Custom configuration providers can be built by implementing IConfigurationSource and IConfigurationProvider.

title: Entity Framework Core Migrations, Model Snapshots, and Rollback
text:
Entity Framework Core migrations provide a code-first approach to database schema evolution. When you modify your entity classes or DbContext configuration, running `dotnet ef migrations add MigrationName` generates a migration file containing Up() and Down() methods that describe the schema changes. The ModelSnapshot file tracks the current state of the model, allowing EF Core to calculate the diff for the next migration.

Applying migrations can be done via CLI with `dotnet ef database update`, programmatically with `context.Database.Migrate()` at startup, or by generating SQL scripts with `dotnet ef migrations script` for DBA-reviewed deployments. The script command supports `--from` and `--to` parameters to generate scripts for specific migration ranges. For production deployments, generating idempotent scripts with `--idempotent` ensures that already-applied migrations are safely skipped.

Rolling back a migration involves running `dotnet ef database update PreviousMigrationName` to apply the Down() method of the unwanted migration. If the migration has already been applied to production, you should create a new migration that reverses the changes rather than removing the migration file. Removing a migration with `dotnet ef migrations remove` only works if it has not been applied to any database. Always test migrations against a copy of the production database before applying them.

Common pitfalls include data loss from dropping columns (add explicit migration steps to preserve data), migration conflicts when multiple developers create migrations simultaneously (resolve by removing the conflicting migration and regenerating), and performance issues with large table alterations. For complex schema changes, break them into multiple migrations: first add the new column with a default value, then backfill data in a separate step, and finally add constraints or remove the old column. EF Core bundles (`dotnet ef migrations bundle`) create a self-contained executable for applying migrations in deployment pipelines.

title: Akka.NET Actor Lifecycle, Supervision, and Message Passing
text:
Akka.NET is a framework for building concurrent, distributed, and fault-tolerant systems using the actor model. An actor is a lightweight processing unit that encapsulates state, behavior, and a mailbox for incoming messages. Actors communicate exclusively through asynchronous message passing, eliminating shared mutable state and the need for locks. Each actor processes one message at a time, providing a natural concurrency model.

The actor lifecycle follows a defined sequence: the actor is created, receives a PreStart signal, processes messages, and can be stopped (receiving PostStop), restarted (receiving PreRestart and PostRestart), or terminated. Actors form hierarchies where parent actors supervise their children. When a child actor throws an exception, the parent's supervision strategy determines the response: Resume (ignore the failure), Restart (recreate the child with fresh state), Stop (terminate the child), or Escalate (pass the decision to the grandparent). OneForOneStrategy applies the decision to the failing child only, while AllForOneStrategy applies it to all children.

Message passing in Akka.NET uses Tell (fire-and-forget) and Ask (request-response) patterns. Tell is preferred because it is non-blocking and does not create back-pressure. Ask returns a Task<T> and should be used sparingly, typically at the boundary between actor code and async/await code. Messages should be immutable records or classes to prevent concurrent modification. The IActorRef reference provides location transparency, meaning the same code works whether the target actor is in the same process or on a remote node via Akka.Remote.

Akka.NET integrates with the .NET dependency injection system through Akka.Hosting. Props define how actors are created, including constructor arguments and deployment configuration. Using `Context.ActorOf(Props.Create<MyActor>(arg1, arg2), "my-actor")` creates a child actor. Actor selection with `Context.ActorSelection("/user/my-service/worker-*")` enables routing messages to groups of actors. For high-throughput scenarios, routers like RoundRobinPool, ConsistentHashingPool, and BroadcastPool distribute messages across pools of worker actors.

title: .NET Dependency Injection: Container, Lifetimes, and Factory Patterns
text:
ASP.NET Core includes a built-in dependency injection (DI) container that manages object creation, lifetime, and dependency resolution. Services are registered in Program.cs using `builder.Services.AddTransient<T>()`, `AddScoped<T>()`, or `AddSingleton<T>()`. Transient services are created each time they are requested. Scoped services are created once per HTTP request (or per scope). Singleton services are created once and shared for the application's lifetime.

Interface-based registration decouples consumers from implementations: `services.AddScoped<IUserRepository, PostgresUserRepository>()` allows controllers to depend on IUserRepository without knowing the concrete implementation. Multiple implementations of the same interface can be registered and resolved using `IEnumerable<IService>` or by using keyed services (introduced in .NET 8) with `AddKeyedScoped<IStorage, S3Storage>("s3")` and `[FromKeyedServices("s3")] IStorage storage` in the constructor.

Factory patterns handle scenarios where service creation requires runtime logic. The `AddScoped<IService>(sp => new MyService(sp.GetRequiredService<IDependency>(), runtimeValue))` overload provides a factory delegate. For more complex cases, an abstract factory pattern or the `IServiceScopeFactory` allows creating new DI scopes on demand, which is essential for background services that need scoped dependencies like DbContext outside of an HTTP request context.

Common DI pitfalls include captive dependencies (a singleton holding a reference to a scoped service, causing the scoped service to live forever), service locator anti-pattern (injecting IServiceProvider and resolving services manually instead of through constructor injection), and excessive constructor parameters (indicating a class has too many responsibilities). The `ValidateScopes` and `ValidateOnBuild` options in the host builder catch these issues at startup. Third-party containers like Autofac and Lamar provide additional features like decorator support, property injection, and assembly scanning.

title: ASP.NET Core Middleware Pipeline: Ordering and Custom Middleware
text:
The ASP.NET Core middleware pipeline processes HTTP requests and responses through a chain of components, each of which can inspect, modify, or short-circuit the request. Middleware ordering is critical because each component sees the request before the next one in the pipeline and sees the response after. The recommended order is: exception handling, HSTS, HTTPS redirection, static files, CORS, authentication, authorization, session, and finally endpoint routing/controllers.

Built-in middleware handles common concerns. `UseExceptionHandler("/Error")` catches unhandled exceptions and returns an error page or problem details JSON. `UseStaticFiles()` serves files from wwwroot without hitting the application code. `UseRouting()` and `UseEndpoints()` (or the simplified `MapControllers()`) configure request routing. `UseRateLimiter()` applies rate limiting policies. Each middleware calls `next(context)` to pass the request to the next component, or it can short-circuit by writing a response without calling next.

Custom middleware is created either as a class with an `InvokeAsync(HttpContext context, RequestDelegate next)` method or as inline middleware using `app.Use(async (context, next) => { ... })`. Class-based middleware is registered with `app.UseMiddleware<MyMiddleware>()` and can accept constructor-injected singleton services and method-injected scoped services. A common use case is request logging middleware that records the request path, duration, status code, and user identity for every request.

Middleware can be conditionally applied using `app.UseWhen(context => context.Request.Path.StartsWithSegments("/api"), appBuilder => { ... })` for branching the pipeline or `app.MapWhen()` for terminal branches. The `Map` method creates pipeline branches based on the request path. For testing, `WebApplicationFactory<TStartup>` and `TestServer` allow testing the entire middleware pipeline in integration tests without running a real HTTP server. Middleware performance should be monitored since each component adds overhead to every request.

title: OpenTelemetry in .NET: Traces, Metrics, and OTLP Exporter
text:
OpenTelemetry (OTel) is the industry-standard framework for collecting traces, metrics, and logs from distributed applications. In .NET, the OpenTelemetry SDK is added via NuGet packages: `OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting`, `OpenTelemetry.Instrumentation.AspNetCore`, `OpenTelemetry.Instrumentation.Http`, and `OpenTelemetry.Exporter.OpenTelemetryProtocol`. Configuration in Program.cs uses the builder pattern to register instrumentation sources and exporters.

Distributed tracing captures the flow of a request across services. ASP.NET Core instrumentation automatically creates spans for incoming HTTP requests, while HTTP client instrumentation traces outgoing calls. Custom spans are created using `ActivitySource.StartActivity("OperationName")` with tags for context: `activity?.SetTag("user.id", userId)`. The W3C TraceContext propagation format ensures trace and span IDs are passed between services via HTTP headers, creating a connected trace that spans multiple services.

Metrics provide aggregate measurements of application behavior. The .NET runtime emits built-in metrics for GC collections, thread pool usage, and HTTP connections. Custom metrics are created with `Meter` and recorded using Counter, Histogram, and ObservableGauge instruments. For example, `_requestCounter.Add(1, new KeyValuePair<string, object?>("endpoint", path))` increments a counter tagged with the endpoint. Metrics are typically scraped by Prometheus or pushed via OTLP to backends like Grafana Mimir or Datadog.

The OTLP exporter sends telemetry data to any OpenTelemetry Collector or compatible backend. Configuration includes the endpoint URL, protocol (gRPC or HTTP/protobuf), headers for authentication, and export interval. The OTel Collector acts as a telemetry pipeline, receiving data from multiple applications, processing it (batching, filtering, sampling), and exporting to one or more backends. A typical setup exports traces to Jaeger or Tempo, metrics to Prometheus, and logs to Loki, all through a single OTel Collector deployment.

title: Serilog Setup, Structured Logging, Log Levels, and Enrichers
text:
Structured logging captures log events as data with named properties rather than simple text strings. Serilog is the most popular structured logging library for .NET, providing message templates that preserve the structure of log data. Instead of `logger.LogInformation($"User {userId} placed order {orderId}")`, structured logging uses `logger.LogInformation("User {UserId} placed order {OrderId}", userId, orderId)`, preserving UserId and OrderId as queryable fields.

Serilog configuration can be done in code or via appsettings.json using the Serilog.Settings.Configuration package. Sinks determine where log events are written: Console, File (with rolling file support), Seq, Elasticsearch, Application Insights, or PostgreSQL. Multiple sinks can be active simultaneously with different minimum levels. For example, write everything Debug and above to a file, but only Warning and above to the console. The `WriteTo.Async()` wrapper prevents logging from blocking the application thread.

Enrichers automatically add contextual properties to every log event. `Enrich.FromLogContext()` enables the LogContext.PushProperty mechanism for ambient properties. `Enrich.WithMachineName()`, `Enrich.WithEnvironmentName()`, and `Enrich.WithThreadId()` add infrastructure context. For web applications, the Serilog.AspNetCore package's `UseSerilogRequestLogging()` middleware replaces the verbose default request logging with a single structured event per request, including method, path, status code, and elapsed milliseconds.

Log levels in Serilog follow the standard hierarchy: Verbose, Debug, Information, Warning, Error, Fatal. Override levels per namespace to reduce noise: set the default to Information but override `Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command` to Warning to silence SQL query logging. In production, use a centralized log aggregation system like Seq, Elastic Stack (ELK), or Grafana Loki that supports searching across structured properties, creating dashboards, and setting up alerts based on error rates or specific log patterns.

title: ASP.NET Core Health Checks: Liveness, Readiness, and Custom Checks
text:
Health checks in ASP.NET Core provide a mechanism for reporting the health of application components. They are configured with `builder.Services.AddHealthChecks()` and exposed via `app.MapHealthChecks()`. Health checks are essential for container orchestrators like Kubernetes, which use liveness and readiness probes to manage pod lifecycle. A failing liveness check causes the pod to be restarted, while a failing readiness check removes the pod from the service load balancer.

The distinction between liveness and readiness is important. Liveness answers the question: is the process running and able to handle requests? It should be a lightweight check, typically just returning healthy if the application can respond. Readiness answers: is the application ready to accept traffic? This includes verifying database connectivity, cache availability, and external service dependencies. Map them to separate endpoints: `MapHealthChecks("/health/live", new HealthCheckOptions { Predicate = check => check.Tags.Contains("live") })` and similarly for readiness.

Custom health checks implement `IHealthCheck` with a `CheckHealthAsync` method that returns HealthCheckResult.Healthy(), Degraded(), or Unhealthy(). Common custom checks include database connectivity (using AspNetCore.HealthChecks.NpgSql for PostgreSQL), Redis availability, disk space thresholds, certificate expiration dates, and external API reachability. The `AddCheck<T>()` method registers custom checks, and tags categorize them for the liveness/readiness endpoints.

Health check UI packages like AspNetCore.HealthChecks.UI provide a dashboard that polls health endpoints and displays historical status. For production monitoring, health check results should be scraped by Prometheus or pushed to Application Insights. Setting appropriate timeouts on health checks (default is 30 seconds) prevents slow dependency checks from timing out the entire health endpoint. The `AddDbContextCheck<TContext>()` extension method provides a quick way to verify Entity Framework Core database connectivity.

title: Application Metrics: Prometheus, Counters, Histograms, and Dashboards
text:
Application metrics provide quantitative measurements of system behavior over time. Unlike logs that capture individual events, metrics aggregate data into time series that are efficient to store, query, and alert on. The four fundamental metric types are counters (monotonically increasing values like request counts), gauges (values that go up and down like active connections), histograms (distributions of values like response latency), and summaries (similar to histograms but calculated client-side).

Prometheus is the most widely adopted metrics system for cloud-native applications. It uses a pull-based model, scraping metrics from application HTTP endpoints at configured intervals. In .NET, the `prometheus-net` library exposes a /metrics endpoint in the Prometheus exposition format. Custom metrics are defined with `Metrics.CreateCounter("myapp_requests_total", "Total requests", new[] { "method", "endpoint" })` and incremented with `.WithLabels("GET", "/api/users").Inc()`. Labels provide dimensions for filtering and grouping in queries.

Histograms are the most valuable metric type for understanding user experience. A histogram for HTTP request duration tracks the distribution of response times across configurable buckets (e.g., 5ms, 10ms, 25ms, 50ms, 100ms, 250ms, 500ms, 1000ms). Prometheus can then calculate percentiles using the `histogram_quantile` function: `histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))` returns the 99th percentile latency over the last 5 minutes. This is far more useful than average latency, which hides tail latency problems.

Grafana dashboards visualize metrics from Prometheus and other data sources. A standard application dashboard includes panels for request rate (requests per second), error rate (percentage of 5xx responses), latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99), and saturation metrics (CPU, memory, connection pool utilization). The RED method (Rate, Errors, Duration) and USE method (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) provide frameworks for choosing which metrics to instrument. Alerting rules in Prometheus or Grafana trigger notifications when metrics cross thresholds, such as error rate exceeding 1% or p99 latency exceeding 500ms.

title: Blazor Component Lifecycle, Parameters, and Event Callbacks
text:
Blazor components are the building blocks of Blazor applications, defined in .razor files that combine C# code with HTML markup. Each component has a lifecycle with well-defined methods: SetParametersAsync (parameters received from parent), OnInitialized/OnInitializedAsync (component first initialized), OnParametersSet/OnParametersSetAsync (parameters set or updated), OnAfterRender/OnAfterRenderAsync (after DOM update), and Dispose (cleanup). Understanding this lifecycle is essential for correctly loading data, managing subscriptions, and avoiding unnecessary re-renders.

Component parameters allow parent components to pass data to children. Parameters are declared as public properties with the [Parameter] attribute. For complex objects, use [CascadingParameter] to pass values down the component tree without explicit parameter passing at each level. RenderFragment parameters enable templated components, and EventCallback<T> parameters allow children to notify parents of events. The pattern `[Parameter] public EventCallback<string> OnSearchChanged { get; set; }` in the child component enables the parent to handle search text changes.

Component rendering optimization is important for performance. By default, Blazor re-renders a component whenever its parent re-renders. Override `ShouldRender()` to return false when the component's state has not changed. The `@key` directive on list items helps Blazor's diffing algorithm preserve DOM elements when list order changes. For expensive operations, use `StateHasChanged()` judiciously to trigger manual re-renders only when necessary, and avoid calling it inside loops.

Component communication patterns include direct parameter/event callback (parent-child), cascading values (ancestor-descendant), dependency-injected services (any components sharing a service), and the built-in NavigationManager for URL-based state. For more complex state management, a centralized state container injected as a scoped service can notify components of changes through events. The pattern of state container with `Action OnChange` events and components subscribing in OnInitialized and unsubscribing in Dispose is widely used in production Blazor applications.

title: CSS Custom Properties, Dark/Light Theme Toggle, and Media Queries
text:
CSS custom properties (CSS variables) provide a powerful mechanism for implementing theme systems. By defining color values, spacing, and typography as custom properties on the `:root` or `html` element, the entire visual appearance of an application can be changed by updating a small set of variables. For example, `--bg-primary: #ffffff` for light mode and `--bg-primary: #1a1a2e` for dark mode, with all components referencing `var(--bg-primary)` instead of hard-coded color values.

Implementing a theme toggle involves a JavaScript function that sets a `data-theme` attribute on the HTML element and persists the choice to localStorage. The CSS uses attribute selectors to apply different variable values: `[data-theme="dark"] { --bg-primary: #1a1a2e; --text-primary: #e0e0e0; }`. The `prefers-color-scheme` media query detects the user's operating system preference and can be used as the default before the user makes an explicit choice. A complete implementation respects the user's saved preference first, then falls back to the OS preference.

Beyond simple color swapping, a well-designed theme system includes semantic color variables rather than literal color names. Variables like `--color-success`, `--color-danger`, `--color-surface`, `--color-on-surface`, and `--color-border` communicate intent rather than specific hues. This makes it easier to maintain accessibility standards because contrast ratios can be verified per theme. Both light and dark themes should meet WCAG 2.1 AA standards with a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text.

Media queries extend theming beyond color to handle responsive design and user preferences. `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` disables animations for users who have indicated this preference. `@media (prefers-contrast: more)` increases contrast beyond the default. `@media (forced-colors: active)` handles Windows High Contrast Mode. Combining CSS custom properties with these media queries creates a flexible, accessible theming system that respects user preferences across all dimensions.

title: Blazor JavaScript Interop, IJSRuntime, and Module Isolation
text:
Blazor's JavaScript interop allows .NET code to call JavaScript functions and vice versa. The IJSRuntime service is injected into components or services and provides `InvokeAsync<T>("functionName", args)` to call global JavaScript functions and receive return values. For fire-and-forget calls, `InvokeVoidAsync` avoids marshaling a return value. JavaScript functions called from Blazor must be accessible on the global window object or imported as modules.

Module isolation, introduced with JavaScript initializers and lazy-loaded modules, prevents global namespace pollution. A component-specific JavaScript file (MyComponent.razor.js) is loaded using `IJSRuntime.InvokeAsync<IJSObjectReference>("import", "./MyComponent.razor.js")`. The returned IJSObjectReference represents the module and supports calling its exported functions. This pattern ensures JavaScript dependencies are loaded only when the component that needs them is rendered, improving initial page load performance.

Calling .NET methods from JavaScript uses DotNetObjectReference to pass a .NET object instance to JavaScript. The JavaScript code calls `dotNetObject.invokeMethodAsync('MethodName', arg1, arg2)` to invoke a [JSInvokable] method on the .NET side. This is useful for JavaScript event handlers, third-party library callbacks, and Web API callbacks like IntersectionObserver or ResizeObserver that need to communicate results back to Blazor components.

Performance considerations for JS interop include minimizing the number of interop calls (batch operations into single calls when possible), avoiding large data transfers across the interop boundary, using `IJSUnmarshalledRuntime` for high-frequency calls in Blazor WebAssembly, and properly disposing IJSObjectReference and DotNetObjectReference instances to prevent memory leaks. For Blazor Server, every JS interop call requires a SignalR round trip, making batching even more important. Common use cases include clipboard access, browser storage, geolocation, and integrating third-party JavaScript libraries like charts, maps, or rich text editors.

title: Azure App Service: Deployment, Scaling, and Application Settings
text:
Azure App Service is a fully managed platform for hosting web applications, REST APIs, and backend services. Deployment options include Git push, GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps Pipelines, ZIP deploy, Docker containers, and FTP. For .NET applications, the most common approach is publishing via `az webapp deploy` or through CI/CD pipelines that build, test, and deploy the application artifact. Deployment slots enable staging deployments that can be swapped to production with zero downtime.

Scaling on App Service operates in two dimensions. Vertical scaling (scaling up) changes the VM size to provide more CPU, memory, and disk I/O. Horizontal scaling (scaling out) adds more instances behind the load balancer. Autoscale rules can be based on metrics like CPU percentage, memory percentage, HTTP queue length, or custom metrics. A typical configuration scales out when average CPU exceeds 70% for 10 minutes and scales in when it drops below 30%. The minimum and maximum instance counts set guardrails on scaling behavior.

Application settings and connection strings in App Service are exposed as environment variables to the running application, overriding any values in appsettings.json. This is the recommended approach for configuration that varies between environments. Settings marked as "deployment slot settings" stay with the slot during swaps, which is useful for slot-specific database connections or feature flags. The Advanced Editor provides JSON import/export of all settings for bulk management.

Diagnostic logging, Application Insights integration, and the Kudu console provide observability into App Service applications. Application Insights provides live metrics, request tracing, dependency tracking, and exception telemetry. The Kudu console (https://myapp.scm.azurewebsites.net) offers a web-based file browser, process explorer, and command-line access for troubleshooting. Health checks configured in the Azure portal automatically remove unhealthy instances from the load balancer rotation, improving reliability during partial outages.

title: AWS EC2, S3, IAM Basics: Getting Started Guide
text:
Amazon Web Services (AWS) provides a vast ecosystem of cloud services. The three most fundamental services are EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) for virtual machines, S3 (Simple Storage Service) for object storage, and IAM (Identity and Access Management) for security and access control. Understanding these three services provides a foundation for working with the broader AWS platform.

EC2 instances are virtual servers available in various sizes (instance types) optimized for different workloads: general purpose (t3, m6i), compute optimized (c6i), memory optimized (r6i), and GPU instances (p4d, g5). Launching an instance requires selecting an Amazon Machine Image (AMI), instance type, VPC and subnet, security group (firewall rules), and key pair for SSH access. User data scripts run at boot time to automate software installation and configuration. For cost optimization, Reserved Instances and Savings Plans provide significant discounts for committed usage, while Spot Instances offer up to 90% savings for fault-tolerant workloads.

S3 stores objects (files up to 5TB) in buckets with a flat namespace that simulates a folder hierarchy using key prefixes. Storage classes (Standard, Intelligent-Tiering, Glacier, and Deep Archive) provide cost optimization based on access frequency. Bucket policies and ACLs control access, and server-side encryption (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, or SSE-C) protects data at rest. Versioning preserves every version of every object, enabling recovery from accidental deletions. Lifecycle policies automatically transition objects between storage classes or delete them after a retention period.

IAM controls who can do what in your AWS account. Users represent individual people, groups organize users with shared permissions, roles provide temporary credentials for services and cross-account access, and policies are JSON documents defining allowed and denied actions. The principle of least privilege should guide policy design. AWS organizations with service control policies (SCPs) provide guardrails across multiple accounts. MFA should be enforced for all human users, especially the root account.

title: Terraform Providers, State Management, Modules, and Plan/Apply
text:
Terraform is an infrastructure-as-code (IaC) tool that enables declarative management of cloud resources. Configuration files (.tf) describe the desired state of infrastructure using HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language). Terraform compares the desired state with the current state and generates a plan showing what changes will be made. The core workflow is `terraform init` (initialize providers and backend), `terraform plan` (preview changes), and `terraform apply` (execute changes).

Providers are plugins that interface with cloud platforms and services. The AWS provider manages EC2, S3, RDS, and hundreds of other AWS resources. The Azure provider (azurerm) handles Azure resources. Providers are declared in a `required_providers` block with version constraints. Each provider is configured with credentials, region, and other settings. Multiple instances of the same provider can be aliased for multi-region or multi-account deployments.

State management is one of Terraform's most critical aspects. The state file maps resource configuration to real-world infrastructure IDs. By default, state is stored locally in terraform.tfstate, but production teams use remote backends like S3 with DynamoDB locking, Azure Blob Storage, Terraform Cloud, or PostgreSQL. Remote state enables team collaboration and prevents concurrent modifications. State should never be committed to version control as it may contain sensitive values. The `terraform state` commands allow manual state manipulation when resources are imported or moved.

Modules encapsulate reusable infrastructure patterns. A module is a directory of .tf files with input variables, output values, and resource definitions. For example, a VPC module might accept CIDR ranges and availability zones as inputs and output subnet IDs and security group IDs. Modules can be sourced from the local filesystem, Git repositories, the Terraform Registry, or S3 buckets. Composing infrastructure from well-tested modules reduces duplication and enforces organizational standards. The `terraform plan` output should always be reviewed carefully before applying, especially for resources with `prevent_destroy` lifecycle rules or those that require replacement (destroy and recreate) rather than in-place updates.

title: DNS Records (A, CNAME, MX, TXT), Propagation, and SSL Certificates
text:
The Domain Name System (DNS) translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses and other resource records. The most common record types are A records (mapping a domain to an IPv4 address), AAAA records (IPv6), CNAME records (alias pointing to another domain name), MX records (mail server routing with priority), TXT records (arbitrary text, often used for domain verification and email security like SPF, DKIM, and DMARC), and NS records (delegating a subdomain to specific name servers).

Configuring DNS for a web application typically involves creating an A record for the apex domain (example.com) pointing to the server's IP address, a CNAME record for www.example.com pointing to example.com, and appropriate MX records for email delivery. If using a CDN or load balancer, the A record may be replaced with an ALIAS or ANAME record (provider-specific solutions for apex CNAME-like behavior) pointing to the CDN's domain. TTL (Time to Live) values control how long resolvers cache records; lower TTLs allow faster propagation during changes but increase query load.

DNS propagation refers to the time it takes for DNS changes to be visible globally. While the change takes effect at the authoritative name server immediately, cached records at recursive resolvers around the world expire based on the previous TTL value. Reducing the TTL several hours before making a critical change ensures faster propagation when the change is applied. Tools like `dig`, `nslookup`, and online DNS propagation checkers help verify that changes have propagated to specific resolvers.

SSL/TLS certificates encrypt the connection between clients and servers. Let's Encrypt provides free certificates via the ACME protocol, with automated renewal through certbot or cloud provider integrations. Certificate validation can be done via HTTP challenge (placing a file at a well-known URL) or DNS challenge (creating a specific TXT record). Wildcard certificates (*.example.com) cover all subdomains and require DNS challenge validation. Cloud providers like AWS Certificate Manager, Azure App Service Managed Certificates, and Cloudflare provide automated certificate management integrated with their CDN and load balancer services.

title: Sprint Planning Meeting Notes, Velocity Tracking, and Backlog Management
text:
Sprint planning is a time-boxed event at the start of each sprint where the team selects work items from the product backlog and creates a plan for delivering them. The product owner presents the highest-priority items, and the development team estimates effort using story points, t-shirt sizes, or ideal hours. The team commits to a sprint goal and a set of backlog items they believe they can complete within the sprint duration, typically two weeks.

Velocity tracking measures the amount of work a team completes per sprint, usually in story points. After several sprints, the average velocity becomes a reliable predictor of future capacity. If a team consistently completes 30-35 story points per sprint, they should plan for approximately 30 points of work. Velocity should not be used to compare teams or as a performance metric, as this leads to point inflation and gaming. It is a planning tool, not a productivity measure.

Backlog refinement (formerly called grooming) is an ongoing activity where the team reviews upcoming backlog items, adds acceptance criteria, breaks large items into smaller stories, and provides rough estimates. Well-refined items make sprint planning more efficient because the team already understands the work. The INVEST criteria (Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable) guide good user story writing. A typical refinement session covers items expected to be planned in the next one to two sprints.

Common sprint planning pitfalls include overcommitting based on optimistic estimates, not accounting for team capacity reductions (vacations, meetings, on-call duties), carrying too much work in progress, and neglecting technical debt items. A healthy sprint backlog includes a mix of feature work, bug fixes, and technical debt reduction. The sprint board (To Do, In Progress, In Review, Done) provides visibility into work status throughout the sprint. Daily standups complement sprint planning by surfacing blockers and enabling rapid course corrections.

title: New Developer Onboarding Checklist, Tool Setup, and Repository Access
text:
A comprehensive onboarding checklist accelerates new developer productivity and ensures consistent team setup. The first day should focus on account provisioning: GitHub organization access, Azure/AWS console access with appropriate IAM roles, CI/CD pipeline access, communication tools (Slack/Teams), project management board access (Jira/Linear), and email/calendar setup. Each account should use SSO where available and have MFA enabled from the start.

Development environment setup includes installing the required SDK versions (.NET 8/9, Node.js LTS), IDE (Visual Studio, Rider, or VS Code with recommended extensions), Docker Desktop, database tools (pgAdmin, Azure Data Studio), and command-line tools (git, gh CLI, az CLI, terraform, kubectl). A setup script or dev container definition automates most of this. The repository README should contain clear instructions for cloning, restoring dependencies, running migrations, and starting the application locally.

Repository access and branching workflow orientation covers the team's Git workflow (trunk-based development vs. feature branches), branch naming conventions, PR template and review requirements, CI/CD pipeline behavior, and deployment process. Pairing the new developer with a buddy for the first two weeks provides a safe channel for questions and accelerates learning of tribal knowledge. The buddy walks through the codebase architecture, explains key design decisions, and helps with the first few pull requests.

The 30-60-90 day plan sets expectations for onboarding milestones. By day 30, the developer should have shipped their first PR, understand the local development workflow, and know the team's ceremonies. By day 60, they should be independently picking up and completing stories from the backlog. By day 90, they should be contributing to design discussions and mentoring newer team members. Regular check-ins during onboarding ensure the new hire feels supported and any gaps in documentation or tooling are identified and addressed.

title: Code Review Best Practices, PR Size, and Review Checklists
text:
Effective code reviews improve code quality, share knowledge across the team, and catch bugs before they reach production. The most important factor in review effectiveness is pull request size. Studies consistently show that review quality drops sharply for PRs over 400 lines of changes. Smaller PRs receive more thorough reviews, get approved faster, and are easier to revert if problems are found. If a feature requires large changes, break it into a series of smaller, reviewable PRs that can be merged incrementally.

A code review checklist helps reviewers consistently evaluate important aspects: Does the code correctly implement the requirements? Are there adequate tests covering the main paths and edge cases? Is the code readable and well-structured? Are there any security concerns (SQL injection, XSS, authentication bypass)? Are error cases handled gracefully? Is the code consistent with existing patterns in the codebase? Are there any performance concerns for the expected data volumes?

Review comments should be constructive, specific, and distinguish between blocking issues and suggestions. Prefixing comments with labels like "nit:" (minor style issue), "question:" (seeking understanding), "suggestion:" (optional improvement), and "blocker:" (must fix before merge) clarifies the reviewer's intent. Reviewers should explain why a change is needed, not just what to change. Positive feedback on good patterns reinforces practices and makes the review process more collaborative.

Automation reduces the burden on human reviewers. Linting, formatting (dotnet format, prettier), static analysis (SonarQube, Roslyn analyzers), and test coverage checks should run in CI before review begins. This ensures reviewers focus on design, logic, and correctness rather than style issues. Required reviewers, CODEOWNERS files, and branch protection rules enforce review policies. The goal is a review culture where reviews happen within hours, not days, and where both author and reviewer view the process as a collaborative improvement rather than a gatekeeping exercise.

title: Italian Pasta Carbonara: Classic Recipe and Technique
text:
Pasta alla carbonara is one of Rome's most iconic dishes, originating from the Lazio region of Italy. The authentic recipe uses just five ingredients: guanciale (cured pork cheek), Pecorino Romano cheese, eggs, black pepper, and pasta (traditionally rigatoni or spaghetti). Despite its simplicity, achieving the perfect creamy sauce without scrambling the eggs requires precise technique and timing.

Begin by cutting 200g of guanciale into strips or small batons. Render the fat slowly in a cold pan over medium-low heat until the guanciale is golden and crispy, about 8-10 minutes. The rendered fat will form the base of the sauce. While the guanciale cooks, bring a large pot of well-salted water to a boil and cook 400g of pasta one minute less than the package instructions for al dente. In a separate bowl, whisk together 4 egg yolks and 2 whole eggs with 100g of finely grated Pecorino Romano and a generous amount of freshly cracked black pepper.

The critical step is combining the hot pasta with the egg mixture without scrambling. Remove the pan with guanciale from direct heat. Add the drained pasta to the guanciale pan (reserve a cup of pasta water). Toss to coat in the rendered fat, then let it cool for 30 seconds. Pour the egg and cheese mixture over the pasta and toss vigorously, adding splashes of starchy pasta water to create an emulsified, silky sauce that clings to each strand. The residual heat from the pasta gently cooks the eggs into a custard-like consistency.

Common mistakes include using cream (never in authentic carbonara), using bacon or pancetta instead of guanciale (they have different flavor profiles), adding the egg mixture while the pan is too hot (causing scrambled eggs), and using Parmigiano Reggiano instead of Pecorino Romano (which provides the characteristic sharp, salty flavor).
